articles.title: Boston Molasses Flood
articles.hook: A storage tank burst in 1919 and sent a wave of molasses through Boston.
articles.summary: A huge molasses tank ruptured in the North End of Boston, flooding streets with a sticky surge that killed people, damaged buildings, and became one of the strangest industrial disasters in American memory.
articles.whyStrange: It sounds like a joke until you read the casualty count, the cleanup, and the lawsuits that followed.

articles.title: London Beer Flood
articles.hook: A brewery burst in 1814 and released a tidal wave of beer into London.
articles.summary: A failed vat at a London brewery triggered a domino effect that sent thousands of gallons of beer into surrounding streets and homes, turning a workday into a disaster that sounded too surreal for reality.
articles.whyStrange: The event is a genuine urban flood, but the floodwater was beer and the city had to treat it like a catastrophe.

articles.title: Great Emu War
articles.hook: Australia sent soldiers against emus and mostly lost.
articles.summary: In Western Australia, the military was deployed to help control emus that were devastating crops. The campaign became a famous example of a state underestimating wildlife and overestimating its own control.
articles.whyStrange: It is a military operation that reads like a parody but is documented as a real administrative decision.

articles.title: Lake Nyos disaster
articles.hook: A lake released a lethal cloud of carbon dioxide overnight.
articles.summary: A limnic eruption at Lake Nyos in Cameroon killed thousands of people and animals when a sudden burst of carbon dioxide rolled through nearby settlements without warning.
articles.whyStrange: The disaster was invisible, odorless, and devastating, which makes it feel like a geological horror story.

articles.title: Dancing plague of 1518
articles.hook: People in Strasbourg danced themselves into exhaustion and death.
articles.summary: In 1518, a wave of uncontrolled dancing spread through Strasbourg, with hundreds of participants reportedly unable to stop. Historians still debate the causes, from mass hysteria to environmental stress.
articles.whyStrange: It is one of the strangest collective episodes in European history because the behavior spread socially, but the exact cause remains uncertain.

articles.title: Dyatlov Pass incident
articles.hook: Nine hikers died in the Ural Mountains under unexplained conditions.
articles.summary: A ski expedition in the northern Urals ended with a tent cut from the inside, scattered bodies, and decades of speculation. The site became a magnet for theories ranging from avalanches to secret military tests.
articles.whyStrange: Its combination of missing context, eerie injuries, and endless theories gives it a mythic quality.

articles.title: The Great Stink
articles.hook: London’s summer heat turned the Thames into a civic emergency.
articles.summary: In 1858, foul sewage odors from the Thames became so severe that Parliament raced to address the city’s sanitation crisis. The episode helped drive one of the most important public-works responses in British history.
articles.whyStrange: A smell problem became a policy shock that transformed infrastructure planning.

articles.title: Project Pluto
articles.hook: The U.S. built plans for a nuclear-powered cruise missile.
articles.summary: Project Pluto explored a ramjet missile powered by a nuclear reactor, promising extreme range and speed at a horrifying cost. It remains one of the most unnerving examples of Cold War engineering ambition.
articles.whyStrange: The project sits at the intersection of brilliant physics and deeply unsettling weapons design.

articles.title: Operation Northwoods
articles.hook: The Pentagon drafted plans for false-flag attacks to justify war.
articles.summary: Operation Northwoods was a proposed set of covert and deceptive actions that would have manufactured support for military intervention. The plan was never carried out, but it became infamous after declassification.
articles.whyStrange: It is astonishing less for what happened than for what was seriously proposed in official channels.

articles.title: Operation Plowshare
articles.hook: The U.S. tried to use nuclear explosions for peacetime engineering.
articles.summary: Operation Plowshare was a broad program for using nuclear detonations to excavate harbors, cut channels, and reshape the land. The technical enthusiasm was enormous; the practical and political objections were even larger.
articles.whyStrange: It is a peace project built on the logic of nuclear blast optimization.

articles.title: Acoustic Kitty
articles.hook: The CIA once tried to turn a cat into a listening device.
articles.summary: Acoustic Kitty was a covert attempt to implant surveillance hardware in a cat so it could eavesdrop on conversations. The project is remembered as an expensive and deeply impractical intelligence experiment.
articles.whyStrange: It is the kind of idea that becomes more absurd the more seriously it was pursued.

articles.title: Project Pigeon
articles.hook: A behaviorist tried to guide missiles with trained pigeons.
articles.summary: Project Pigeon proposed using pigeons to visually steer guidance systems inside missiles. The concept came from serious experimental psychology and remains one of the most memorable examples of wartime improvisation.
articles.whyStrange: It combines animal training, engineering optimism, and military seriousness in a way that is hard to forget.

articles.title: Casaba-Howitzer
articles.hook: A nuclear weapon concept aimed to turn blast energy into a beam.
articles.summary: Casaba-Howitzer was a speculative nuclear weapon design that would shape energy into a focused directional pulse. The concept lives at the edge of physics, weapons engineering, and military imagination.
articles.whyStrange: It sounds like a fantasy prop, but it emerged from real Cold War research logic.

articles.title: Orion nuclear pulse propulsion
articles.hook: NASA once studied spacecraft propelled by nuclear explosions.
articles.summary: Project Orion proposed a gigantic spacecraft pushed forward by repeated nuclear detonations behind a pusher plate. The idea was technically serious, audacious, and almost unimaginable by contemporary standards.
articles.whyStrange: It is one of the most spectacular examples of engineering at the far edge of the possible.

articles.title: Duga radar
articles.hook: The Soviet Union built a giant over-the-horizon radar array.
articles.summary: Duga was an enormous Soviet radar installation whose electrical noise led listeners to nick it the Russian Woodpecker. It remains an iconic artifact of the Cold War landscape.
articles.whyStrange: It is both a listening machine and a haunting piece of industrial architecture.

articles.title: Project Habakkuk
articles.hook: Britain once explored an aircraft carrier made of ice.
articles.summary: Project Habakkuk proposed using reinforced ice as a material for enormous wartime naval platforms. The plan was part engineering problem, part material-science experiment, and part emergency improvisation.
articles.whyStrange: An ice carrier is such a strange sentence that the project feels fictional until you trace the documents.

articles.title: Biosphere 2
articles.hook: A sealed habitat in Arizona tried to simulate a self-sustaining world.
articles.summary: Biosphere 2 was a massive experimental enclosure designed to test closed ecological systems and long-term human habitation. The project became famous for technical setbacks, internal tensions, and the ambition of trying to bottle a biosphere.
articles.whyStrange: It is a scientific experiment that looks like a utopian cult and acts like both a lab and a stage set.

articles.title: Milgram experiment
articles.hook: People were asked to deliver shocks to strangers in the name of research.
articles.summary: Stanley Milgram’s obedience experiments are famous for revealing how readily ordinary participants might follow authority even when the task seems morally troubling. The experiments remain central to psychology and ethics debates.
articles.whyStrange: The setup is simple, but the implications about obedience and agency are deeply unsettling.

articles.title: Stanford prison experiment
articles.hook: A fake prison study quickly turned into a lesson in power dynamics.
articles.summary: The Stanford prison experiment simulated a prison environment to examine how role assignment and institutional power might influence behavior. It became one of the most debated studies in social psychology.
articles.whyStrange: Its legacy is not just the data, but the way the experiment itself became a cautionary tale.

articles.title: The Third Wave
articles.hook: A classroom experiment created a miniature authoritarian movement.
articles.summary: The Third Wave was a high-school classroom experiment meant to demonstrate how quickly fascistic dynamics could take root in a group. It produced a disturbing burst of conformity and remains a vivid teaching example.
articles.whyStrange: An educational exercise briefly became a real social movement, which is exactly the kind of line the atlas loves.

articles.title: Fordlândia
articles.hook: Henry Ford tried to build a rubber utopia in the Amazon.
articles.summary: Fordlândia was a company town and plantation project meant to secure rubber supply for Ford’s industrial empire. Instead it became a notorious story of ecological mismatch, paternalism, and failed planning.
articles.whyStrange: It is what happens when a car company tries to export Midwestern order into the rainforest.

articles.title: Auroville
articles.hook: A planned city in India aimed to become a universal township.
articles.summary: Auroville was founded as an intentional community with spiritual and social ambitions that stretched far beyond a conventional town plan. Its history includes idealism, governance disputes, and the difficulty of turning utopian ideas into durable institutions.
articles.whyStrange: It is a city that was explicitly designed to be more than a city.

articles.title: Kowloon Walled City
articles.hook: A dense enclave in Hong Kong became an urban labyrinth.
articles.summary: Kowloon Walled City was a compact, heavily built-up settlement known for its density, informal governance, and maze-like architecture. It became an enduring icon of urban extremity.
articles.whyStrange: It looks like a city designed by layering until the geometry gave up.

articles.title: Hashima Island
articles.hook: An abandoned coal island became a concrete ghost ship.
articles.summary: Hashima Island, off the coast of Nagasaki, was once a dense mining settlement before industrial decline left it largely deserted. Its silhouette and history made it famous as a symbol of modern ruin.
articles.whyStrange: It is an island that looks like a ship, a fortress, and a ruin all at once.

articles.title: Pripyat
articles.hook: A Soviet city near Chernobyl was abandoned almost overnight.
articles.summary: Pripyat was built to house workers for the Chernobyl plant and was evacuated after the reactor disaster in 1986. It remains one of the most recognizable abandoned cities in the world.
articles.whyStrange: It is a planned city frozen at the instant its purpose ended.

articles.title: Centralia, Pennsylvania
articles.hook: A mine fire turned a town into a long-burning evacuation zone.
articles.summary: An underground coal seam fire in Centralia has burned for decades, making the borough nearly uninhabitable. The town became a haunting example of slow, persistent industrial catastrophe.
articles.whyStrange: The ground itself became the enemy, and the fire never really stopped.

articles.title: Nakagin Capsule Tower
articles.hook: A modular Tokyo tower became a shrine to metabolist architecture.
articles.summary: Nakagin Capsule Tower was built from detachable prefabricated units as a vision of flexible urban living. It later became beloved as a strange, fragile icon of architectural futurism.
articles.whyStrange: It is an apartment building that looks like an exploded camera roll of the future.

articles.title: Pruitt-Igoe
articles.hook: A modernist housing project collapsed into a symbol of urban failure.
articles.summary: Pruitt-Igoe was a large public-housing complex in St. Louis whose demolition became shorthand for the failures of midcentury planning, policy, and maintenance.
articles.whyStrange: Its destruction became more famous than its life, which is a brutal kind of legacy.

articles.title: Buzludzha Monument
articles.hook: A Bulgarian monument resembles a crashed spaceship on a mountain.
articles.summary: The Buzludzha Monument was built as a communist memorial and now stands as a decaying concrete spectacle. Its form and deterioration make it one of the most striking ruins of the Eastern Bloc.
articles.whyStrange: It looks like a sci-fi prop left in the mountains after filming wrapped.

articles.title: Ryugyong Hotel
articles.hook: Pyongyang’s giant pyramid hotel became a monument to unfinished ambition.
articles.summary: The Ryugyong Hotel has towered over Pyongyang for decades as a largely dormant megastructure. It is one of the most famous examples of architecture becoming political spectacle.
articles.whyStrange: It is a skyline-defining building that spent years being more promise than function.

articles.title: Sealand
articles.hook: A sea fort off England became a self-declared micronation.
articles.summary: Sealand is a former wartime platform that was turned into a pseudo-state with its own titles, flags, and mythology. It remains one of the strangest acts of political self-invention.
articles.whyStrange: It is statehood as performance art, but with concrete and sea spray.

articles.title: Republic of Rose Island
articles.hook: An offshore platform briefly declared itself a nation.
articles.summary: The Republic of Rose Island was an engineered platform in the Adriatic that was proclaimed a sovereign state in the 1960s. It became a legendary example of playful, defiant micronationalism.
articles.whyStrange: It is what happens when libertarian fantasy meets maritime engineering.

articles.title: Heaven's Gate
articles.hook: A cult of the apocalypse expected to exit the planet in a spacecraft.
articles.summary: Heaven's Gate was a religious movement that blended UFO beliefs, communal living, and apocalyptic expectation. Its tragic end made it one of the most infamous cult stories in modern memory.
articles.whyStrange: Its cosmology was so specific that it almost feels fabricated after the fact.

articles.title: Church of the SubGenius
articles.hook: A parody religion became a real subculture.
articles.summary: The Church of the SubGenius began as a satirical media prank and evolved into a durable, playful religious parody with its own iconography and lore.
articles.whyStrange: It is one of the best examples of a joke becoming an institution.

articles.title: Colonia Dignidad
articles.hook: A secluded colony in Chile became notorious for abuse and violence.
articles.summary: Colonia Dignidad was a settlement founded by German expatriates that later became associated with human rights abuses, authoritarian control, and political collaboration.
articles.whyStrange: It is a community with a deeply sinister hidden history.

articles.title: Cargo cult
articles.hook: Imitation ritual emerged around the arrival of modern goods.
articles.summary: Cargo cults are social and religious movements that developed in parts of Melanesia in response to colonial encounters and sudden material change.
articles.whyStrange: The term sounds dismissive, but the underlying history is far more complex.

articles.title: Surtsey
articles.hook: A volcanic island emerged from the sea off Iceland.
articles.summary: Surtsey formed in the 1960s and has been tightly protected for scientific study ever since. It is a rare place where geology, ecology, and conservation all meet in real time.
articles.whyStrange: It is a brand-new island that immediately became an experiment in not interfering.

articles.title: Cardiff Giant
articles.hook: A fake petrified giant fooled 19th-century crowds.
articles.summary: The Cardiff Giant was an elaborate hoax created in New York and displayed as a fossilized giant human. It became a celebrated example of American curiosity and credulity.
articles.whyStrange: The joke was so successful that people paid money to believe it.

articles.title: Cottingley Fairies
articles.hook: Two girls produced photographs of fairies that captivated the world.
articles.summary: The Cottingley Fairies were a famous early-20th-century photographic hoax that fooled many observers, including some prominent believers in spiritualism.
articles.whyStrange: It is a tiny local prank that grew into an international belief drama.

articles.title: John Frum
articles.hook: A cargo cult centered on a mysterious promised figure.
articles.summary: John Frum is a legendary figure associated with cargo beliefs and political symbolism in parts of Vanuatu. The name became a lasting cultural symbol with layers of colonial and local meaning.
articles.whyStrange: It is a belief system built around a name that may itself be a clue, joke, or mythic placeholder.

articles.title: Taman Shud case
articles.hook: An unidentified man and a cryptic note became a long-running mystery.
articles.summary: The Taman Shud case centers on an unknown deceased man found in Adelaide and the fragment of poetry linked to him. The case has generated decades of speculation and investigative fascination.
articles.whyStrange: It has all the ingredients of a spy thriller without ever resolving into one.

articles.title: Isdal Woman
articles.hook: An unidentified woman was found dead in Norway under strange circumstances.
articles.summary: The Isdal Woman case remains an unresolved mystery involving aliases, burned belongings, and conflicting clues about her identity and movements.
articles.whyStrange: The investigation keeps opening new doors without ever reaching a final room.

articles.title: Pyramiden
articles.hook: A Soviet mining town on Svalbard became a frozen monument to abandonment.
articles.summary: Pyramiden was a remote Arctic settlement with industrial ambitions, later abandoned after Soviet-era decline. Today it stands as one of the most atmospheric ghost towns in the North.
articles.whyStrange: It feels like someone left a planned city in the freezer and never came back.

articles.title: Clock of the Long Now
articles.hook: A clock is being designed to tick for ten thousand years.
articles.summary: The Clock of the Long Now is a long-term thinking project focused on building a mechanical clock intended to run for millennia. It is as much philosophy and art as engineering.
articles.whyStrange: It is a machine built to make time feel bigger than human institutions.

articles.title: The Great Moon Hoax
articles.hook: Newspaper reports claimed life had been discovered on the Moon.
articles.summary: The Great Moon Hoax was a series of fake newspaper stories that described elaborate lunar life. It is a classic example of 19th-century media sensationalism.
articles.whyStrange: A fabricated astronomy story once passed as public fact and made headlines.

articles.title: Zzyzx, California
articles.hook: A desert settlement was named to be the last word in the dictionary.
articles.summary: Zzyzx began as an offbeat desert development and health resort with a deliberately memorable name. It has become a roadside curiosity and a symbol of eccentric American branding.
articles.whyStrange: It is a place that sounds invented because, in a sense, it was.

articles.title: Mellified man
articles.hook: A medieval legend claimed a human body could be preserved in honey and sold as medicine.
articles.summary: The mellified man is a legendary medicinal preparation described in historical sources as a human corpse slowly transformed into a honey-based cure. The story sits at the edge of pharmacology, folklore, and a kind of beautifully unsettling alchemy.
articles.whyStrange: It is one of those stories that becomes stranger the more seriously it is described by the sources.

articles.title: Cadaver Synod
articles.hook: A pope was exhumed and put on trial nine months after death.
articles.summary: The Cadaver Synod was a ninth-century ecclesiastical trial in which the corpse of Pope Formosus was exhumed, dressed, and judged in Rome. The spectacle turned church politics into one of history’s most surreal legal proceedings.
articles.whyStrange: A corpse on trial is already bizarre; the historical record makes it somehow more vivid.

articles.title: God helmet
articles.hook: A magnetic helmet was used to probe religious experience and altered states.
articles.summary: The God helmet is an experimental apparatus built to study the neural correlates of creativity, presence, and spirituality. It became famous because the setup sounds mystical, but the project was really an attempt to measure cognition with a machine that looks half theatrical prop, half laboratory hardware.
articles.whyStrange: It is a serious research device with a name that feels like it escaped from a science-fiction script.

articles.title: Monster Study
articles.hook: An experiment on stuttering left a dark trail through the history of speech research.
articles.summary: The Monster Study was a 1939 experiment in which children were subjected to harsh speech feedback in an attempt to study stuttering. It became infamous both for its methodology and for the way it lingers as a warning about research ethics.
articles.whyStrange: Its nickname is memorable, but the real story is even more uncomfortable than the label suggests.

articles.title: Island of the Dolls
articles.hook: A chinampa in Mexico City became a shrine of dolls, legend, and dark tourism.
articles.summary: The Island of the Dolls is a tiny island in Xochimilco where dolls were hung from trees and structures for decades, creating one of the strangest and most photographed destinations in Mexico City. The site’s reputation comes from local legend, eerie visual density, and the sheer persistence of its haunting image.
articles.whyStrange: It is a place built from ordinary objects arranged until they become unsettling.

articles.title: Bhangarh Fort
articles.hook: A ruined fort in Rajasthan became famous as one of India’s most haunted places.
articles.summary: Bhangarh Fort is a 16th-century ruin wrapped in folklore, local legend, and official warnings that only deepen its reputation. It is less about a single event than about how a place can accrue myth until the ruin itself feels narrated.
articles.whyStrange: The fort is visually dramatic on its own, but its reputation has become part of the architecture.

articles.title: Green children of Woolpit
articles.hook: Two children reportedly appeared in medieval England with green skin.
articles.summary: The legend of the green children of Woolpit tells of a brother and sister who emerged in Suffolk speaking an unknown language and looking unlike anyone around them. The story has endured because it sits right on the seam between folklore, historical record, and literary mystery.
articles.whyStrange: A medieval account of green-skinned children is strange enough; the competing explanations are stranger still.

articles.title: Mokele-mbembe
articles.hook: A Congo Basin cryptid became a long-running hunt for a living dinosaur.
articles.summary: Mokele-mbembe is a legendary water-dwelling creature reported in Central African folklore and later chased by cryptozoologists hoping to turn myth into zoology. The idea kept resurfacing because it sits right on the edge of indigenous story, colonial curiosity, and scientific wishful thinking.
articles.whyStrange: It is a dinosaur-shaped absence that keeps attracting serious expeditions.

articles.title: The Hum
articles.hook: Some people hear a low frequency drone that nobody else can quite place.
articles.summary: The Hum is a persistent low-frequency sound reported in different places around the world, often with no obvious source and no consensus explanation. Its power comes from the fact that it is half environmental mystery and half private sensory experience.
articles.whyStrange: A sound that is real for some people and absent for others makes uncertainty feel audible.

articles.title: Phantom time conspiracy theory
articles.hook: A fringe theory claims centuries of history were invented or shifted into place.
articles.summary: The phantom time hypothesis argues that parts of the early medieval period were fabricated or misdated, creating a kind of historical glitch in the calendar. It survives as a reminder that chronology itself can become a site of conspiracy thinking.
articles.whyStrange: It tries to make history feel counterfeit by moving whole centuries around.

articles.title: Shroud of Turin
articles.hook: A relic with a faint image has spent centuries producing arguments and devotion.
articles.summary: The Shroud of Turin is a linen cloth bearing an image that has inspired both veneration and intense scientific scrutiny. Its enduring strange power comes from how the relic lives between evidence, faith, image analysis, and public fascination.
articles.whyStrange: It is an object whose mystery survives every attempt to turn it into a simple answer.

articles.title: The Langley Schools Music Project
articles.hook: Children’s choir recordings became a cult object decades after they were made.
articles.summary: The Langley Schools Music Project consists of amateur school recordings from the 1970s that were rediscovered and turned into a beloved artifact of outsider music. The project feels uncanny because it preserves a very specific moment of childhood, arrangement, and aspiration in audio form.
articles.whyStrange: It is tender, eerie, and strangely monumental all at once.

articles.title: Dracula gigas
articles.hook: An orchid named for a monster has a flower that looks back at you.
articles.summary: Dracula gigas is an orchid species whose common name, monkey orchid, comes from the uncanny resemblance of its bloom to a primate face. It belongs to the family of plants that seem to be doing face-making as a side effect of evolution.
articles.whyStrange: A flower that resembles a little creature is already strange; one named Dracula is stranger still.

articles.title: Monkey orchid
articles.hook: A European orchid blooms in a shape that suggests a tiny monkey.
articles.summary: Orchis simia is commonly called the monkey orchid because its flower lip resembles a small simian figure. It is the kind of botanical oddity that seems designed to make taxonomic language feel playful.
articles.whyStrange: Nature quietly produces something that looks like a miniature character sketch.

articles.title: Red mercury
articles.hook: A black-market rumor about a mysterious substance kept fueling nuclear paranoia.
articles.summary: Red mercury is a discredited alleged material that circulated in arms-smuggling and espionage lore as something valuable, secret, and potentially nuclear-adjacent. Its endurance says as much about fear, rumor, and black-market storytelling as it does about chemistry.
articles.whyStrange: It is a phantom commodity that survived because secrecy made it hard to disprove.

articles.title: Disappearance of Harold Holt
articles.hook: Australia’s prime minister vanished while swimming, and the country never stopped asking why.
articles.summary: Harold Holt disappeared in 1967 near Cheviot Beach and was presumed drowned, but the event spawned an enduring cloud of folklore, speculation, and national memory. The story remains strange because a routine tragedy turned into a permanent cultural shorthand.
articles.whyStrange: A sitting prime minister disappearing into the sea sounds like fiction until you reach the archival record.

articles.title: Hollow Earth
articles.hook: A disproven theory imagined the planet as a hidden interior world.
articles.summary: The Hollow Earth idea proposed that our planet contains a vast hollow interior, and it persisted for centuries across pseudoscience, folklore, and adventure fiction. It is one of those concepts that survives because it is bigger than evidence and far more cinematic than geology.
articles.whyStrange: The earth itself becomes a stage set for secret civilizations and lost entrances.

articles.title: Shag Harbour UFO incident
articles.hook: A reported crash into the sea turned into one of Canada’s best-known UFO cases.
articles.summary: In 1967, witnesses near Shag Harbour, Nova Scotia, reported a mysterious lit object descending into the water, prompting official search efforts that found no debris and no explanation. The incident remains memorable because the paperwork, witness accounts, and missing object never fully reconcile.
articles.whyStrange: It is a government-filed mystery with ocean water where the evidence should have been.

articles.title: Bell Witch
articles.hook: A Tennessee family became the center of one of America’s most persistent haunting legends.
articles.summary: The Bell Witch legend describes a long-running alleged haunting that attached itself to a real family, a real place, and a lot of later retellings. The story endures because it feels simultaneously local, folkloric, and weirdly procedural.
articles.whyStrange: It is a haunting that keeps acquiring new layers of cultural memory.

articles.title: Utsuro-bune
articles.hook: A Japanese tale described a strange vessel and an enigmatic woman washed ashore.
articles.summary: Utsuro-bune is a nineteenth-century Japanese legend involving a mysterious boat and a figure who may be a folkloric visitor, an alien, or simply an accretion of story. Its endurance comes from how it sits between maritime legend, coded imagery, and UFO-era reinterpretation.
articles.whyStrange: It is a folkloric encounter that keeps being retold as something else.

articles.title: Mummy brown
articles.hook: Painters once used a pigment made from mummified remains.
articles.summary: Mummy brown was a historical pigment produced from ground mummies and related materials, prized for its warm transparency before its origins became more widely understood. It is a perfect example of art history being stranger once the supply chain is revealed.
articles.whyStrange: A color on a palette can turn into a human-remains story very quickly.

collections.title: Disasters that sound made up
collections.description: Events where the premise alone seems fictional: floods of food and drink, invisible gas clouds, and towns that became cautionary tales.

collections.title: Cold War weirdness
collections.description: Secret plans, nuclear fantasies, and projects that feel like science fiction smuggled into state paperwork.

collections.title: Failed utopias
collections.description: Grand social visions, planned cities, and corporate dreams that ran into ecology, power, or the simple friction of reality.

collections.title: Social experiments
collections.description: Behavioral studies, classroom simulations, and communities that tried to engineer human nature from first principles.

collections.title: Strange places
collections.description: Abandoned cities, island anomalies, oversized monuments, and settlements that became famous for how they look and feel.

collections.title: Myths and mysteries
collections.description: Cases, hoaxes, and unexplained events that keep generating theories because the documents are incomplete and the story is too good to die.

collections.title: Things governments seriously funded
collections.description: These are the pages that make you reread the source material twice because the official record is stranger than the joke version.

collections.title: Vanished technologies
collections.description: Technologies and systems that almost won, stalled out, or left behind beautiful ruins and better stories than products.

collections.title: Eccentric rich people projects
collections.description: Large personal visions that became expensive, strange, and almost impossible to separate from the people who made them.

collections.title: Bizarre legal cases
collections.description: Unusual cold cases, strange evidence trails, and stories where legal ambiguity is part of the mystery.

collections.title: Absurd history
collections.description: Historical episodes that are real, well documented, and still feel like someone made them up on purpose.

collections.title: Infrastructure that mattered
collections.description: Public works and systems that were ugly, costly, necessary, or all three at once.

collections.title: Science dead ends
collections.description: Ideas that were once considered plausible enough to fund, test, or build before they were overtaken by reality.

collections.title: Cults and charismatics
collections.description: Movements, personalities, and belief systems that blur the line between performance, devotion, and social control.

collections.title: Micronations
collections.description: Tiny claims of sovereignty, whether playful, ideological, or legally inventive.

collections.title: Deranged architecture
collections.description: Buildings and urban forms that look impossible, overscaled, or too strange to have been fully intentional.

collections.title: Small-print curiosities
collections.description: A drawer of tiny, hard-to-disbelieve stories that feel like they were filed away rather than featured.

collections.title: Haunted footnotes
collections.description: Legends, ruins, and procedural oddities that become stranger when you read the details twice.

collections.title: Afterimage files
collections.description: A second drawer for relics, hums, cryptids, and recorded oddities that keep echoing after the first glance.

collections.title: Shadow dossiers
collections.description: A deeper drawer for rumors, disappearances, UFO reports, and objects whose explanation remains half a story.

themes.label: Weird science
themes.description: A route from odd experiments into the machinery of escalation.

themes.label: Political scandal
themes.description: A route through secret plans, public failures, and hard-to-believe decisions.

themes.label: Strange places
themes.description: A route through vanished cities, monuments, and impossible settlements.

themes.label: Social experiments
themes.description: A route through experiments, cults, and engineered communities.

themes.label: Made-up disasters
themes.description: A route through events that sound like fiction but were terrifyingly real.
